test_cases.prompt:
Analyze this codebase for cleanup. Identify all issues including dead code, unnecessary abstractions, AI slop patterns, and duplicated logic. Prioritize findings by impact.

{fixture:1-ai-slop-codebase}
expected_behaviors: Identifies DatabaseManager as unnecessary wrapper around sqlite3.connect()
Identifies StringHelper as unnecessary wrapper around str.strip()
Identifies try/except/raise as catch-rethrow with no added value
Identifies excessive comments explaining obvious operations
Identifies process_data and handle_data as duplicated functions
Identifies 3 unused imports (os, sys, json) as dead code
Identifies commented-out function as dead code

test_cases.prompt:
Analyze this codebase for cleanup. Identify all issues including security vulnerabilities, dead code, unnecessary abstractions, and code quality problems. Prioritize findings by impact.

{fixture:2-hidden-security-in-mess}
expected_behaviors: Identifies command injection via os.system with user input as highest-priority finding
Prioritizes security finding above all style and slop issues
Identifies os.system with f-string interpolation as the attack vector

test_cases.prompt:
Analyze this codebase for cleanup. Identify all issues including dead code, unnecessary abstractions, AI slop patterns, and duplicated logic. Report only genuine issues.

{fixture:3-clean-codebase}
expected_behaviors: No significant issues exist in the code
Code is well-structured with appropriate abstractions
Docstrings and error handling are appropriate, not excessive

test_cases.prompt:
Analyze this codebase for cleanup. Identify all issues including dead code, unnecessary abstractions, AI slop patterns, and duplicated logic. Be precise: only flag genuine issues, not intentional design patterns.

{fixture:4-mixed-real-and-slop}
expected_behaviors: Identifies unused import (re) as dead code
Identifies catch-rethrow as unnecessary
Does NOT flag similar-named functions as duplicates (they have different logic)
Does NOT flag connection pool wrapper as unnecessary (it adds real value)

test_cases.prompt:
Analyze this codebase for cleanup opportunities. Identify all issues, prioritize by severity, and propose a cleanup plan.

{fixture:5-dirty-app-full-codebase}
expected_behaviors: Identifies at least 7 of the 9 planted issues across the codebase
Classifies security issues (SSRF, injection, JWT confusion) as critical/high severity
Identifies the TOCTOU race as a concurrency/correctness issue
Does NOT flag validators.py — it is clean code

test_cases.prompt:
Analyze this codebase for cleanup opportunities. Identify all issues, prioritize by severity, and propose a cleanup plan.

{fixture:6-clean-app-full-codebase}
expected_behaviors: Recognizes the codebase follows security best practices
Does NOT fabricate security vulnerabilities in well-written code
Does NOT flag os.environ['SECRET_KEY'] as a hardcoded secret
Does NOT flag the broad except in the error handler as an issue
May suggest minor improvements but marks them as non-blocking
Reports only minor style observations (quote inconsistency, unused variable, verbose docstrings)
Does NOT flag any security vulnerabilities — there are none
Does NOT flag docstrings as AI slop — they are just verbose
Does NOT invent architectural concerns or dead code patterns
Finds the cross-module coupling: services/payment.py imports models/user.py's private _hash_card() helper
Finds the cross-module duplication: identical discount calculation logic in api/orders.py and api/returns.py
Finds the 8 obvious issues (dead code, unused imports, catch-rethrow patterns)
Distinguishes within-module issues from cross-module issues
Identifies convention drift across files — some use snake_case, some use camelCase
Recommends standardizing on the dominant convention (snake_case)
Rejects the ADR-031 'multi-style' adversarial claim — codebase shows a clear dominant pattern
Identifies inconsistent error handling and import ordering

test_cases.prompt:
Analyze this codebase for cleanup. Identify all issues including dead code, unnecessary abstractions, AI slop patterns, and duplicated logic. Report only genuine issues.

{fixture:10-unfamiliar-legitimate-patterns}
expected_behaviors: Does NOT flag RegistryMeta metaclass as unnecessary abstraction — it is a standard Python auto-registration pattern
Does NOT flag FieldDescriptor as AI slop — it is a standard Python descriptor protocol implementation (PEP 487)
Does NOT flag __init_subclass__ as dead code — it is the registration hook called by the Python runtime
Does NOT flag __set_name__ as dead code — it is part of the descriptor protocol
Identifies the unused import: typing_extensions is imported but never used
Identifies the catch-rethrow pattern would be valid if it existed but this code does not have one — does not fabricate it
Does NOT flag the ABC/abstractmethod pattern as unnecessary complexity — it enforces interface contracts

test_cases.prompt:
Analyze this codebase for cleanup. Identify all issues, classify them by category, and prioritize by impact.

{fixture:11-ambiguous-category-boundaries}
expected_behaviors: Classifies format_response() and ResponseFormatter as dead code — no callers exist
Classifies validate_user_input() and sanitize_user_data() as duplicates — identical logic
Classifies buildQuery() as BOTH a security issue (SQL injection via string interpolation) AND a convention violation (camelCase)
Prioritizes buildQuery SQL injection above all other findings — security trumps style
Flags camelCase naming on buildQuery and formatTimestamp as convention violations against the dominant snake_case pattern
Flags bare except in DataProcessor.process() as a convention violation — other code uses specific exceptions
Does NOT flag retry_with_backoff() as dead code — it is a legitimate utility
Does NOT flag DataProcessor.transform() returning NotImplemented as dead code — it is an ABC pattern

test_cases.prompt:
Analyze this codebase for cleanup. Identify all issues including dead code, unnecessary abstractions, security vulnerabilities, duplicated logic, and convention violations. Report only genuine issues — do not flag legitimate Python patterns.

{fixture:12-advanced-patterns-with-real-issues}
expected_behaviors: Identifies eval() in execute_handler as a critical security vulnerability — command injection via user input
Identifies LEGACY_HANDLERS dict and _format_v1_response() as dead code
Identifies serialize_user() and format_user_for_api() as exact duplicates
Identifies AbstractBaseHandlerFactory as unnecessary abstraction — single concrete subclass, no other factories
Identifies mixed sync/async convention (process_webhook vs process_event do the same thing)
Does NOT flag ValidatedField descriptor as dead code or unnecessary — __set_name__ is PEP 487
Does NOT flag GenericModel.__class_getitem__ as dead code — it is standard typing
Does NOT flag EventHandler.__init_subclass__ as unnecessary — it is the plugin registry pattern
Does NOT flag ConfigManager.settings cached_property as dead code — it is lazy initialization
Does NOT flag managed_connection asynccontextmanager as incorrect — yielding a different object is correct
Prioritizes eval() security issue above all other findings
